item.title: Report on Elevated Lung Cancer Fails to Quell Uproar Around New York’s Largest Landfill
item.description: [Stoicism & Philosophy] Keyword: Seneca — This article previously appeared in Water Front.  SENECA FALLS, N.Y.—In its recent 20-page report on elevated lung cancer rates around Seneca Meadows Inc., the state Department of Health (DOH) officially declares that the state’s largest municipal waste landfill deserves no blame. That conclusion has hardly soothed those who have long complained of sickening odors and respiratory ailments that have […]

item.title: Western Europe Just Had Its Hottest June on Record — Climate Change Is to Blame
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — Excess deaths in France, Belgium, Spain, and the Netherlands last month exceeded 4,600.

item.title: Data Centers Are Building Power Plants Near Neighborhoods—That May Play Into Some South San Antonio Ambitions
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — While some Texas cities have advanced bans and moratoriums on data centers until the risks are better understood, San Antonio has proceeded more cautiously toward possible regulation. And some see economic opportunity.

item.title: Texas Biomed to help Pentagon develop defenses against emerging biothreats
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — Texas Biomedical Research Institute is partnering with the U.S. Department of Defense to protect soldiers and citizens from biothreats, including natural infectious diseases and the emerging threat of lab-engineered bioweapons. Texas Biomed to help Pentagon develop defenses against emerging biothreats was first posted on July 9, 2026 at 2:52 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report". item.title: "We Want Texans to Know Their Rights": Q&A with Mayday Health on the Impact of Surveillance on Abortion Care
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — Last May, EFF reported that a sheriff’s office in Texas searched data from more than 83,000 automated license plate reader (ALPR) cameras to track down a woman suspected of self-managing an abortion. ALPRs are promoted as tools for keeping communities safe by finding missing persons and locating stolen vehicles, but this case showed how ALPRS can be weaponized to investigate people’s private healthcare decisions. And these aren’t the only tools in the surveillance arsenal: others include locatio

item.title: Florida Regulators Stood by as Dozens of Sloths Died, New Records Reveal
item.description: [Ecology & Conservation] Keyword: wildlife conservation — The first warning reached the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission before a single sloth had arrived in Orlando.  On Dec. 9, 2024, a member of the public called the agency after seeing advertisements for a new business called Sloth World. The caller said owner Benjamin Agresta was hiring employees and telling prospective workers that […]

item.title: Heat waves: Why British trees are shedding branches and dying
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — If you visit the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew, on the edge of London, you will see a brightly painted skeleton of a dead oak tree. The tree, known as the climate-changed oak, succumbed in the heat wave of 2022. Instead of removing it, Kew left it in place as a reminder that climate change is already taking its toll on Britain's trees.

item.title: UK swelters in third heatwave of the year as western Europe counts cost of hottest-ever June
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Britain expands heat alerts while estimates suggest June’s death toll could surpass 20,000 across continentThe UK is sweltering through the peak of its third heatwave of the year as countries around Europe struggle to recover from an early onslaught of baking summer heat.Punishing temperatures pushed higher by fossil fuel pollution have broken records across the continent in recent weeks. Western Europe experienced its hottest June on record, scientists confirmed on Thursday, accompanied by high

item.title: Earthquakes in Venezuela expose a severely under-resourced and unprepared healthcare system
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — Two physicians discuss the ongoing earthquake relief efforts in their home country of Venezuela and explain what the healthcare system was like before the disaster.

item.title: The color of penguin poo: Satellites reveal global warming's impact on an iconic polar species
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: global warming — Scientists from a handful of universities across the country have made innovative use of satellite images from NASA to determine the diet of Antarctic Adélie penguins across the continent by studying their icy feces with a fidelity and frequency that could be captured only by space technology.

item.title: Fossils found decades ago reveal extinct 3.5 million-year-old giant salamander species
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — In the late 1990s in the Ajimu region of Japan's Oita Prefecture, researchers discovered three fossilized vertebrae belonging to the Cryptobranchidae family of giant salamanders. These were embedded in the Tsubusugawa Formation, Pliocene-era strata of lake deposits dating back approximately 3.5 million years. The strata have also yielded fossils of animals that no longer roam Japan, such as elephants and crocodiles, revealing a glimpse of an era much warmer and more humid than Japan's current cl

item.title: What Is (and Isn’t) Happening with the Border Wall in Big Bend
item.description: [Immigration & Border] Keyword: border wall — You’ve probably heard about the Trump administration’s plans for a border wall through Big Bend. Or its plans not to build a wall at all, but instead to put up a futuristic forcefield of lights and sensors. You may have even heard that public pressure backed the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) into a corner […] The post What Is (and Isn’t) Happening with the Border Wall in Big Bend appeared first on The Texas Observer.

item.title: Fife & Farro pizza-making, Mariachi Nights head busy July calendar at Pullman Market
item.description: [AI & Automation] Keyword: LLM — Pizza-making classes taught by Fife & Farro executive chef Josh Davis and Mariachi Nights at the Mezcalaria headline a busy month at Pullman Market.Fife & Farro pizza-making, Mariachi Nights head busy July calendar at Pullman Market was first posted on July 9, 2026 at 10:00 am.©2021 "San Antonio Report". item.title: Farmers Finally Get a John Deere Right to Repair Agreement That Doesn’t Screw Them Over
item.description: [Digital Rights & Privacy] Keyword: right to repair — The FTC's settlement with John Deere actually has teeth, unlike previous settlements that largely maintained the status quo.

item.title: Why removing a distinct religious code for Native American military service members will make their needs invisible
item.description: [Indigenous News & Tribal Sovereignty] Keyword: Indigenous — The Pentagon recategorized Native religions as ‘other.’ A scholar of Native American and Indigenous Studies explains the implications of that change.

item.title: Species’ ingenious survival strategies no match for human destruction, red list reveals
item.description: [Ecology & Conservation] Keyword: endangered species — Newly endangered animals include desert frogs and snails in extreme ocean depths, both threatened by miningLife has colonised every corner of the planet by evolving ingenious survival strategies but these are increasingly being overwhelmed by destructive human activities, this year’s red list of endangered species has revealed.Many snails, limpets and clams have adapted to life at crushing depths in the oceans on hydrothermal vents where water temperatures can reach 450C (842F). But an assessmen

item.title: This Mars rover could finally reveal whether life ever existed on Mars
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — The hunt for ancient life on Mars just got an important test run. Scientists confirmed that the Rosalind Franklin rover's sophisticated instrument can detect subtle differences in two stable molecules that could preserve evidence of past life for billions of years. But the team also uncovered a surprise: organic molecules in the Murchison meteorite appear to have been contaminated by fossil fuel pollution during their journey through Earth's atmosphere.

item.title: [SAPD] Burglary Vehicle — 1200 KNIGHTS CROSS DR (NORTH)
item.description: [SAPD Calls for Service] Keyword: Burglary Vehicle — Incident #SAPD-2026-0930227 | NORTH Division | 7/9/2026 4:48:53 AM

item.title: How to build a highway in the age of climate change
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change —

item.title: Ancient fossil may reveal animal kingdom's earliest right-handedness at 550 million years old
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Scientists have uncovered what may be the earliest evidence of "right-handedness" in the animal kingdom, dating back more than half a billion years. The discovery comes from the fossil record of Spriggina floundersi, an organism from the Ediacaran Period that lived about 550 million years ago.

item.title: The Iran war has pushed some countries away from oil and toward clean energy
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — The new round of bombing in the Middle East has underscored the precarity of relying on fossil fuels. The war is speeding up the global transition to EVs, solar, and batteries, experts say.

item.title: Organizer from the conservative Americans for Prosperity nominated to serve on appraisal board
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — Jonathan "Jon" Melendez, a grassroots engagement director at Americans for Prosperity, was among 15 candidates who applied for a temporary appointment after BCAD board member Robert “Bob” Bruce died in March.Organizer from the conservative Americans for Prosperity nominated to serve on appraisal board was first posted on July 9, 2026 at 5:00 am.©2021 "San Antonio Report". item.title: Rare goblin shark filmed alive for the first time in the deep sea
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — For the first time, researchers have filmed the elusive goblin shark alive in the deep ocean where it naturally lives. The remarkable sightings greatly expand the shark's known range and depth, showing that this 125-million-year-old "living fossil" still has plenty of secrets left to reveal.

item.title: [$] LWN.net Weekly Edition for July 9, 2026
item.description:
[AI & Automation] Keyword: LLM — Inside this week's LWN.net Weekly Edition:
Front: Cryptography API; Iomap explanation; Negative dentries; Faster RCUs and lockless allocation for BPF; Negative dentries; LLMs in memory-management code Briefs: Guix vulnerabilities; OpenSSH 10.4; trusted publishing; kernel archive; CalyxOS; Quotes; ... Announcements: Newsletters, conferences, security updates, patches, and more.

item.title: Confirmed Ebola deaths in DR Congo hit 600
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — The number of deaths comes as healthcare workers threaten to walk off the job over a delay in payments.

item.title: Decoding of one of nature's largest enzymes reveals electron flow behind biological methane production
item.description: [Science & Research] Keyword: microbiology — A research team at Marburg University has investigated one of the largest enzyme complexes found in nature to date and deciphered its remarkable structure. Under the supervision of Dr. Jan Schuller, Ph.D. student Sophia Paul from the Center for Synthetic Microbiology (SYNMIKRO) was able to characterize the so-called heterodisulfide reductase super-assembly in detail. The results of the study are now published in the journal Nature. They show how a molecular "giant" comprising hundreds of buildin

item.title: Indigenous peoples in the Amazon face massive cultural and ecological loss due to climate change
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — The Amazon region, Earth's most important ecosystem, is home to more than 400 Indigenous groups that use thousands of rainforest plant species. They pass on their knowledge of the flora primarily through oral tradition, usually from parents or other family members to their children. This creates a "living library of knowledge" about how to use native plants. Until now, little was known about how this treasure trove of knowledge is affected by the combined effects of climate change and language l

item.description: [SAPD Calls for Service] Keyword: Assault In Progress — Incident #SAPD-2026-0927677 | CENTRAL Division | 7/8/2026 3:51:40 PM

item.title: Woman accused of stealing ambulance while medics treated patient in downtown San Antonio
item.description: [Texas Surveillance] Keyword: San Antonio police — San Antonio police arrested a woman accused of stealing an ambulance while two paramedics treated a patient in the back. Woman accused of stealing ambulance while medics treated patient in downtown San Antonio was first posted on July 8, 2026 at 3:14 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report". Use of this feed is for personal non-commercial use only. If you are not reading this article in your feed reader, then the site is guilty of copyright infringement. Please contact me at hello@rivardrepo

item.title: International team says science alone won't save coral reefs
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — Coral reefs are disappearing at an unprecedented rate as climate change, marine heat waves, pollution and coastal development threaten one of Earth's richest ecosystems. While scientific research has greatly advanced understanding of the crisis and ways to restore damaged reefs, a new international paper argues that science alone will not be enough to protect them.

item.title: Assistant Chief Jesse Salame named acting San Antonio police chief
item.description: [Texas Surveillance] Keyword: San Antonio police — Jesus “Jesse” Salame Jr. will serve as acting San Antonio police chief as the city searches for a permanent replacement for William McManus.Assistant Chief Jesse Salame named acting San Antonio police chief was first posted on July 8, 2026 at 1:15 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report". Use of this feed is for personal non-commercial use only. If you are not reading this article in your feed reader, then the site is guilty of copyright infringement. Please contact me at hello@rivardreport.

item.title: How Fourth of July celebrations and the national political mood may shape psychedelic experiences
item.description: [Psychedelics & Psilocybin Research] Keyword: psychedelic medicine — Psychedelic drugs are known to make people highly sensitive to their surroundings. In other words, a user's mindset and immediate environment heavily shape the entire trippy experience. In a study published in the journal Psychedelic Medicine, scientists wanted to test a brand-new idea: whether an invisible backdrop of national culture, rather than just a person's local setting, could influence people's support for partisan violence after taking a psychedelic.

item.title: [SAPD] Assault — SW LOOP 410 / CARMONA PASS (SOUTH)
item.description: [SAPD Calls for Service] Keyword: Assault — Incident #SAPD-2026-0926737 | SOUTH Division | 7/8/2026 12:34:30 PM

item.description: [SAPD Calls for Service] Keyword: Burglary Vehicle — Incident #SAPD-2026-0926741 | WEST Division | 7/8/2026 12:35:10 PM

item.title: Large language model guides discovery of catalysts for clean energy tech
item.description: [AI & Automation] Keyword: large language model — Designing high-performance catalysts is essential for cleaner energy technologies, but the behavior of multi-element modern catalyst materials is difficult to predict. In a new study, researchers at Tohoku University with international collaborators developed a collaborative framework that combines large language models with lab experiments to accelerate the discovery of high-entropy alloy catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction, a key process in fuel cells.

item.title: This rare British butterfly looks familiar, but its genome tells a very different story
item.description: [Ecology & Conservation] Keyword: endangered species — The British swallowtail butterfly (Papilio machaon britannicus) is the U.K.'s only native swallowtail and its largest native butterfly. It's instantly recognizable by its striking light yellow-and-black wings, with twin tail-like markings splashed in bold blue and red. Habitat for this endangered species has largely been limited to the damp bogs and marshes of eastern England, especially the iconic Norfolk Broads, where its life cycle is closely tied to a single host plant, milk-parsley.

item.title: Social norms can accelerate or undermine climate action, new model finds
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — A new mathematical model suggests that social norms may be just as important as economics in determining how the world responds to climate change. The research shows that efforts to reduce emissions in one region can unintentionally influence climate action elsewhere, with consequences that could either strengthen or weaken global progress.

item.title: Oil prices rise sharply after Iran launches attacks on tankers near strait of Hormuz
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Brent crude benchmark rose to more than $78 a barrel, its steepest increase since ceasefire beganOil markets have recorded the sharpest price rise in nearly two months after a series of attacks on fossil fuel tankers near the strait of Hormuz led Donald Trump to declare that the ceasefire deal with Iran was “over”.Brent, the global crude benchmark rose by 5% on Wednesday to more than $78 (£58) a barrel, the highest price since the US and Iran agreed the ceasefire while negotiating an end to the

item.description: [SAPD Calls for Service] Keyword: Burglary — Incident #SAPD-2026-0925681 | CENTRAL Division | 7/8/2026 8:28:27 AM

item.title: Trump plan to appease MAHA’s fury backfired big time
item.description: [Small Farm & Urban Agriculture] Keyword: regenerative agriculture — On a 200-acre farm and cattle ranch in Bandera, Texas, Mollie Engelhart grows organic produce, sells raw milk, and writes a daily column about the power of regenerative agriculture. She’s a farmer and a Make America Healthy Again mom who doesn’t like being called a MAHA mom. She prefers to think of herself as “MAHA-aligned.” In May, Engelhart opened her ranch to a couple hundred pro-MAHA politicians, activists, and leaders for a two-day MAHA farming retreat. Department of Health and Human Servi

item.title: Tiny Jurassic bird reveals a key step in bird evolution
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — The transition from a lumbering, heavy dinosaur body to the flight-adapted bird body plan is one of many fascinating episodes in evolutionary history. Working out how this massive transformation took place relies heavily on fossil records, especially of transitional species. A study of a newly identified Jurassic bird published in the journal Science Advances is providing fresh insights into a tail adaptation that helps birds fly.

item.title: A spirit of resilience helps Venezuelans face healthcare challenges after the quakes
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — The economic collapse of Venezuela has pushed its healthcare system to the brink. The disaster is now adding to the stress.

item.title: [SAPD] Missing Person/Runaway — 200 NOLAN ST (DOWNTOWN)
item.description: [SAPD Calls for Service] Keyword: Missing Person/Runaway — Incident #SAPD-2026-0925251 | DOWNTOWN Division | 7/8/2026 4:36:43 AM

item.title: Hotter, drier weather could double water bills in some US cities, study finds
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — Hotter, drier weather threatens to double water bills by midcentury in some cities, according to a Stanford-led study. The research, published in Nature Sustainability, is the first to comprehensively model how climate change, infrastructure investment and household water demand can combine to compound an already growing affordability crisis.

item.title: Climate Change Is Helping an Invasive Predator Wreak Havoc on Iconic Alaskan Fish
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — WILLOW, Alaska—Corey Ercolani pulled a northern pike from a gillnet and slit its belly with a knife. Inside its guts lay fresh evidence of a growing biological crime: a dead juvenile salmon. A coho, or silver salmon, to be exact. A technician with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, Ercolani had set the net […]

item.title: The oldest deliberately collected fossil ichthyosaur was discovered in Roman Britain around 1,800 years ago
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Around 1,800 years ago, a fossilized spinal bone lay on the windswept beaches of Roman Britain until a curious passerby picked it up and carried it far away, only to drop it in a pit.

item.title: The great carbon capture con: behold the wasted billions Burnham could claw back | George Monbiot
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — There are far better ways to tackle climate breakdown, but successive governments have chosen to listen to the fossil fuel companies insteadThe new prime minister will be looking for money? Well, here’s £21.7bn lying on the ground. The government could cancel its deranged, disastrous carbon capture and storage (CCS) programme at no cost to public welfare: in fact, it would greatly reduce the harm we will suffer.Sorry, did I say £21.7bn? That’s the figure the government has been putting in its pr

item.title: New weight loss pill beats oral Ozempic in major trial
item.description: [Diabetes & Kidney Disease] Keyword: blood sugar — A new once-daily weight-loss pill called orforglipron delivered better weight loss and blood sugar improvements than the leading oral semaglutide in a major clinical trial. The tablet could offer a more convenient alternative to injectable drugs like Ozempic and Wegovy because it doesn't require refrigeration or special timing with meals. It's also cheaper to manufacture, which could expand access globally.

item.title: Women and university graduates in Australia most at risk of losing jobs to AI, report finds
item.description: [Housing & Gentrification] Keyword: displacement — Those with high levels of vocational training, including tradespeople, are least exposed to AI displacement, according to government reviewGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastArtificial intelligence has yet to cause widespread job losses but the federal government has warned that telemarketers, advertising staff and accountants are among the occupations “most exposed” to being replaced by the technology.According to a first-of-its-kind national report, people in the more e

item.title: Warehouse store plants corridor’s first retail flag as TxDOT works to keep up with growth
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — Construction on the busy interchange continues with a flyover underway amid the ensuing commercial and residential growth.Warehouse store plants corridor’s first retail flag as TxDOT works to keep up with growth was first posted on July 8, 2026 at 5:00 am.©2021 "San Antonio Report". Use of this feed is for personal non-commercial use only. If you are not reading this article in your feed reader, then the site is guilty of copyright infringement. Please contact me at hello@ri

item.title: How climate change affects interactions between owls and their prey
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — A study published in Ecography has assessed how climate change may be destabilizing interactions between predators and prey in the wild—specifically, how owl–prey interactions have responded to environmental variability and resource availability over 24 years in the semi-arid ecosystem of Bosque Fray Jorge National Park in Chile.

item.title: UK museums at 'sharp end' of climate change challenge
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — As visitors peered at skeletons and preserved animals in a London zoological museum during a UK heat wave, staff focused on a different attraction: a computer screen glowing with red and green temperature indicators.

item.title: Health risk fears for storks in Europe over ‘junk food’ from landfill
item.description: [Foraging & Wild Plants] Keyword: foraging — Rubbish dumps can expose birds to contaminants, raising questions over whether landfill foraging helps or harmsStorks are gaining weight from a diet of literal junk, according to research that suggests the previously disappearing birds face potential health risks as a result of increasingly eating from rubbish dumps.Landfill offers what appear to be quick and convenient meals for white stork populations in Europe. But new research suggests they may be gaining a short-term energy boost at the cos

item.description: [SAPD Calls for Service] Keyword: Burglary in Progress — Incident #SAPD-2026-0924817 | CENTRAL Division | 7/8/2026 12:35:02 AM

item.title: How tall and short trees can coexist in old growth forests
item.description: [Ecology & Conservation] Keyword: old growth forest — Forests are shaped by light competition. The trees that grow the tallest have access to the most sunlight, blocking the rays and rendering the shaded space around them inhospitable to shorter trees below. In this stem exclusion phase of forest succession, the shorter trees often die. Yet scientists have observed that in old-growth forests, trees of vastly different sizes successfully coexist, proving that reaching the top of the canopy is not the sole winning strategy for survival in a forest en

item.title: Global warming, increasing wildfire risk threaten viability of some California winery regions
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: global warming — The U.S. is the fourth-largest wine-producing country by output volume, and approximately 80% of its production occurs in California. Ever since the 19th century, California's premier wine-growing regions have been the Napa Valley and Sonoma County, thanks to their favorable microclimate. But grape yield and quality are very sensitive to the local environment, which means the climate crisis could shake up California's wine industry.

item.title: Where rivers face collapse: New tool shows where conservation dollars can do most good
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: biodiversity loss — Freshwater ecosystems are under growing pressure worldwide, but conservation resources are limited. A framework developed by IIASA researchers and partners can help identify where conservation could prevent biodiversity loss and where restoration efforts are likely to have the greatest ecological impact across the United States and Europe.

item.title: Bexar County moves to spend $10M on South Beach Pavilion project for Defense Health Agency
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — Additional funding of $10 million for the renovation will come from the City, and $5 million in state grants, for a total of $25 million.Bexar County moves to spend $10M on South Beach Pavilion project for Defense Health Agency was first posted on July 7, 2026 at 6:09 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report". item.title: Satellite record reveals US tidal wetland productivity rose 6% in 20 years
item.description: [Climate & Environment] Keyword: climate change — Carbon sequestration, climate regulation, biodiversity support and shoreline protection: These are all benefits provided by tidal wetlands. As the climate changes, the amount of carbon captured by these vital ecosystems may be changing as well.

item.title: Unique fossil record of marine mollusks helps scientists predict extinction risk—before it's too late
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — Scientists are increasingly worried we may be witnessing the start of the "sixth mass extinction"—the first to be caused by human activities.

item.title: Spurs get permission for more international games, plus up to 2 Austin ‘home’ games per season
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — Bexar County leaders granted the San Antonio Spurs permission to play four regular-season home games outside of the Frost Bank Center during each of the 2026-2027 and 2027-2028 NBA seasons.Spurs get permission for more international games, plus up to 2 Austin ‘home’ games per season was first posted on July 7, 2026 at 4:29 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report". Use of this feed is for personal non-commercial use only. If you are not reading this article in your feed reader, th

item.title: Record Heat Drives Wildfires in Europe, Forces Thousands to Evacuate
item.description: [Cannabis, Hemp & Drug Policy] Keyword: THCA — Wildfires are tearing through southern France and parts of Spain, Portugal and Greece, forcing thousands of people to evacuate from their homes. The wildfires follow a record-breaking June heat wave across the continent that caused thousands of deaths, strained healthcare facilities, dried out acres of land and is slated to continue with triple-digit temperatures in […]

item.title: Trump Tells Big Tech He’ll Fast-Track Fossil Fuel Plants to Power AI
item.description: [Open Source Software] Keyword: FOSS — The AI development race has resulted in plans to build or expand 74 methane gas plants across the country.

item.title: Justice Department scrambles to save Todd Blanche from himself
item.description: [Government Accountability] Keyword: FOIA — The Justice Department is currently trying to save acting Attorney General Todd Blanche from a lawsuit filed by the government watchdog group American Oversight over the release of files related to Jeffrey Epstein.In June, the group filed suit with a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request involving “all email communications sent or received by Todd Blanche and containing both a key term from Column A and a key term from Column B,” and “all text messages and messages on messaging platforms ..

item.title: CineFestival returns with its largest showcase yet of San Antonio filmmakers
item.description: [Texas & San Antonio Local] Keyword: San Antonio — The 47th CineFestival San Antonio returns July 9-12 with award-winning films, free screenings and one of its largest showcases of San Antonio filmmakers.CineFestival returns with its largest showcase yet of San Antonio filmmakers was first posted on July 7, 2026 at 12:50 pm.©2021 "San Antonio Report".
